_provenance: Claude-authored original Kaoyan (English I) style reading, batch F5 (final, 8 sets to bring kaoyan reading lv5 to 50). ~300-350 word abstract argumentative passages, 4 MCQs each. Topics distinct from F1-F4. DeepSeek not used.

sets.passage: There is a widespread assumption that doubt is a weakness and certainty a strength, that the confident mind is the strong one and the hesitant mind the feeble. In the conduct of inquiry, this assumption is precisely inverted, and the failure to see as much lies behind much bad thinking. For certainty, far from being the natural reward of knowledge, is more often the mark of its absence. The person sure of everything has typically examined little; his confidence is purchased by a refusal to consider the difficulties that would unsettle it, and the very strength of his conviction measures the narrowness of his acquaintance with the question. The expert, by contrast, who has lived with a problem and felt its full complexity, is characteristically the more tentative, aware of how much remains unsettled and how easily he might be wrong. Genuine understanding, it turns out, tends to multiply the occasions for doubt rather than to dispel them. This is not to recommend a paralysing scepticism that doubts everything and concludes nothing; such a posture is as useless as the dogmatism it opposes, and in its own way as lazy. The doubt that serves inquiry is not a refusal to believe but a refusal to believe more firmly than the evidence warrants — a calibration of conviction to its grounds, holding tentative conclusions tentatively and firm ones firmly. It is, in short, intellectual humility: the disposition to remain open to correction, to treat one's current beliefs as the best available rather than the final word, and to welcome the challenge that might improve them. Far from a weakness, this is the precondition of all learning, for the mind closed by certainty has nothing left to learn, while the mind that holds its beliefs with appropriate doubt remains, alone, educable. Far from being the enemy of conviction, this disciplined doubt is its only honest foundation; the mind that holds nothing open to revision has merely confused the strength of its feelings with the strength of its evidence, and has closed, in the name of certainty, the one door through which understanding could ever enter.

questions.prompt: What 'widespread assumption' does the writer invert?
options: That doubt is a weakness and certainty a strength.

questions.prompt: Why is certainty 'more often the mark of [knowledge's] absence'?
options: Because the certain have examined everything.
Because confidence is purchased by refusing to consider unsettling difficulties.

questions.prompt: What kind of doubt does the writer recommend?
options: A paralysing scepticism that concludes nothing.
A refusal to believe more firmly than the evidence warrants.
A refusal to believe anything at all.

questions.prompt: Why is intellectual humility 'the precondition of all learning'?
options: Because the certain mind learns fastest.
Because the mind closed by certainty has nothing left to learn.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 颠倒'怀疑是弱、确定是强'之假设)、细节(B 确定常因拒绝考虑会动摇它的难处而买来、故是无知之标)、细节(B 推荐之怀疑=不比证据所许更坚定地相信)、主旨(B 被确定封闭的心智无可再学、故谦逊是学习前提)。

sets.passage: We readily acknowledge obligations to those around us — to family, neighbours, fellow citizens — but obligations to people who do not yet exist, to the generations that will inherit the world after we are gone, present a puzzle that our ordinary moral thinking is poorly equipped to handle. That we have such obligations few would flatly deny; the idea that we may use up the earth, exhaust its resources and destabilise its climate, leaving our descendants a despoiled and diminished world, strikes most people as plainly wrong. Yet when we try to give this intuition a firm foundation, peculiar difficulties arise. The beneficiaries of our restraint do not yet exist, and cannot claim their due, press their case, or reciprocate our care; they are wholly at our mercy, unable to affect us in return. The ordinary machinery of obligation, which leans heavily on reciprocity and on the claims of identifiable persons, finds no purchase here, and a narrow self-interest, consulting only those who can reward or punish us, would discount the future entirely. And yet to follow that narrow logic would be monstrous, and we know it. The obligation to the future, if it cannot be derived from reciprocity, must rest on something else: on a recognition that the moral worth of persons does not depend on their power to affect us, that those who will suffer from our heedlessness are no less real for being distant in time, and that we are, whether we like it or not, the trustees of a world we did not make and do not own. To act as though the future did not matter because it cannot retaliate is to confess that our morality extends no further than our self-interest — a confession that the better part of us refuses to make, and that the gravest challenges of the age will not permit us to make for long. To accept our role as the trustees of a world we will never live to see is, in the end, among the truest measures of a moral seriousness equal to the demands of the age, and a refusal to make it is one our gravest problems will not much longer indulge.

questions.prompt: Why do obligations to future generations 'present a puzzle'?
options: Because no one believes in them.
Because our ordinary moral thinking, leaning on reciprocity, is poorly equipped for them.
Because the future can reward us.
Because they are identical to present obligations.

questions.prompt: What feature of future beneficiaries creates the difficulty?
options: They can press their case forcefully.
They cannot claim their due, reciprocate, or affect us in return.

questions.prompt: On what must the obligation to the future rest, if not reciprocity?
options: On the power of the future to retaliate.
On the recognition that moral worth does not depend on power to affect us.

questions.prompt: What would acting 'as though the future did not matter' confess?
options: That our morality extends no further than our self-interest.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 后代义务成谜:依赖互惠的常识道德难应付)、细节(B 后代无法索取、回报或反作用于我们)、细节(B 该义务须立于'道德价值不取决于影响我们的能力')、主旨/态度(B 视未来无足轻重等于承认道德不出自利之外)。

sets.passage: Among the more useful illusions by which human beings live is the sense that they are in control of their lives — that their fortunes are, in the main, the product of their own decisions, and that by wise choice they may steer their course to a destination of their choosing. The illusion is useful because it sustains effort and responsibility; the person who believes his actions matter is more likely to act well than one who feels himself the plaything of forces beyond his reach. But an illusion it largely is, and the wisdom of maturity lies partly in recognising how much of what befalls us lies outside our governance. Consider how thoroughly the shape of any life is determined by circumstances no one chose: the era and place of one's birth, the family and the genes one inherits, the chance encounters that open or close decisive doors, the accidents of health and history that no foresight could anticipate. Against this vast inheritance of the given, the scope of deliberate choice, though real, is narrower than our self-flattering narratives admit. To grasp this is not to sink into fatalism or to abandon the effort that remains genuinely ours. It is, rather, to exchange a brittle illusion for a sturdier wisdom — one that takes responsibility for the choices that are truly choices while accepting, with a measure of grace, the vast remainder that is not. The person who imagines himself the sole author of his fate is set up for a peculiar bitterness when fortune, as it must, defies him; he can only conclude that he has failed. The person who has understood the limits of his control meets the same reverses with greater equanimity, having never mistaken the small territory of his agency for the whole kingdom of his life. There is, in this acceptance, not weakness but a hard-won strength. To accept the limits of our control is therefore not to surrender our agency but to place it where it can actually do some good, husbanding our finite powers for the few things that truly answer to them, and meeting the vast remainder, which answers to no one, with a steadiness that the illusion of mastery could never supply.

questions.prompt: Why does the writer call the sense of control a 'useful illusion'?
options: Because it sustains effort and responsibility, though it is largely an illusion.

questions.prompt: What determines 'the shape of any life', according to the writer?
options: Circumstances no one chose: era, family, genes, chance encounters, accidents.

questions.prompt: What does grasping the limits of control NOT amount to?
options: Sinking into fatalism or abandoning genuine effort.

questions.prompt: How does the person who understands his limits meet reverses?
options: With greater equanimity, never mistaking his agency for his whole life.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 控制感是有用的幻觉:维系努力与责任、但多为幻觉)、细节(B 人生形貌由无人选择之境遇决定)、细节(B 认清局限不等于陷入宿命或弃真努力)、主旨/态度(B 懂得局限者以更大平静面对逆境)。

sets.passage: The pressure to conform to the opinions of those around us is among the most powerful forces shaping human belief, and among the least acknowledged, for it works most effectively when we are least aware of it. We like to think of our convictions as the products of our own reasoning, arrived at by an independent weighing of evidence. In truth, the great majority of what any of us believes is absorbed, unexamined, from the surrounding consensus, adopted less because we have found it true than because those around us hold it, and to dissent would be uncomfortable. Famous experiments have demonstrated how far this pressure can carry us: individuals will deny the plain evidence of their senses, affirming what they can see to be false, rather than stand alone against a unanimous group. The conformist impulse is not, in itself, contemptible; a creature that trusted nothing it had not personally verified could not survive, and deference to the collective wisdom is often reasonable. But the same impulse that usefully transmits sound belief transmits error with equal efficiency, and a whole society may be carried, by the mutual reinforcement of its members, into convictions that no individual, reasoning freely, would have reached. The independent thinker — the one who can entertain a conclusion his community rejects, and resist a conclusion it embraces — is therefore rarer and more valuable than the easy celebration of independent thinking suggests. Genuine independence of mind is not a natural endowment but a hard discipline, demanding a tolerance for the discomfort of dissent that few possess; and the societies that prize it must work to protect it, for the pressure to conform, left to itself, will always tend to extinguish the very independence on which the correction of collective error depends. To resist the pull of conformity is among the hardest and most valuable of disciplines, for the pressure to agree works most powerfully when it is least noticed; a society that prizes the independent mind must therefore protect it deliberately, since the faculty by which collective error is corrected is the first that an unchecked consensus will quietly extinguish.

questions.prompt: Why is conformity 'among the least acknowledged' forces?
options: Because it works most effectively when we are least aware of it.

questions.prompt: What did the 'famous experiments' demonstrate?
options: That people always trust their senses.
That individuals will deny plain evidence rather than stand alone against a unanimous group.

questions.prompt: What does the writer say the conformist impulse does 'with equal efficiency'?
options: Transmits error as efficiently as it transmits sound belief.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's view of genuine independence of mind?
options: A natural endowment most people have.
A hard discipline, rare and valuable, that societies must work to protect.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 从众最少被承认:在我们最无察觉时最有效)、细节(B 著名实验:人宁否认眼见之实也不愿独抗一致群体)、细节(B 从众同样高效地传播错误)、主旨(B 真正独立是稀有而宝贵的硬纪律、社会须设法保护)。

sets.passage: Our culture's attitude towards failure is strikingly confused. On the one hand, we proclaim, in countless inspirational maxims, that failure is the road to success, that we learn more from our defeats than our victories, that the path to achievement is paved with stumbles bravely overcome. On the other hand, our actual practice belies these comforting slogans: we stigmatise the failed, reward only the successful, and arrange our institutions so as to punish error rather than to mine it for its lessons. The contradiction is worth examining, for the truth about failure is more demanding than either the slogans or the practice allows. Failure is not, in itself, valuable; the romantic notion that defeat automatically ennobles or instructs is sentimental nonsense, and most failure teaches nothing but discouragement. What is valuable is not failure but the disciplined response to it — the willingness to examine a defeat unflinchingly, to extract from it the lesson it contains, and to carry that lesson forward rather than fleeing the painful memory. This response is rare precisely because it is difficult; it runs against the natural impulse to forget, to excuse, to blame circumstance, and it demands a painful honesty about one's own shortcomings. The cultures and individuals that genuinely profit from failure are those that have built the structures — psychological and institutional — that make this honest reckoning possible: that treat error not as a disgrace to be hidden but as information to be studied, and that can distinguish the blameless failure of an honest attempt from the culpable failure of negligence. To learn from failure, in short, is neither automatic nor easy, but a hard-won achievement; and a society that mouths the slogans while punishing the reality has secured for itself the worst of both, neither the comfort of forgiving failure nor the benefit of learning from it. To learn from failure well is thus neither automatic nor easy but a hard-won achievement, demanding an honesty about our own mistakes that every instinct resists; the cultures and individuals that genuinely improve are those that have learned to treat a failure not as a disgrace to be hidden but as information to be studied and, in time, surpassed.

questions.prompt: What 'contradiction' does the writer identify in our attitude to failure?
options: We both praise and learn from failure.
We proclaim failure is the road to success, yet stigmatise and punish the failed.

questions.prompt: What does the writer say is NOT valuable in itself?
options: Failure itself, which mostly teaches only discouragement.

questions.prompt: What actually is valuable, according to the writer?
options: The disciplined response that examines defeat and extracts its lesson.

questions.prompt: What has a society that 'mouths the slogans while punishing the reality' secured?
options: The worst of both: neither forgiving failure nor learning from it.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 矛盾:口称失败是成功之路、实则污名并惩罚失败者)、细节(B 失败本身无价值、多只教人沮丧)、细节(B 有价值的是审视失败、提取教训的有纪律回应)、主旨(B 只喊口号却惩罚现实的社会两头落空)。

sets.passage: Human beings do not, on the whole, understand the world through abstract propositions; they understand it through stories. Long before we reason in arguments, we make sense of experience by casting it into narrative — into sequences of events linked by cause and motive, peopled by agents with purposes, shaped towards a meaning. This narrative habit is so deep that it operates beneath our awareness, structuring our memory, our sense of ourselves, our grasp of history and of other minds. To understand a person is to know his story; to make sense of a life, our own included, is to find the thread of narrative that runs through its disconnected moments. The power of this mode of understanding is immense, and largely benign. Narrative renders the booming confusion of experience intelligible, gives us purchase on the behaviour of others, and transmits across generations a wisdom that no list of propositions could convey. Much of what is deepest in human culture is carried in stories, and the capacity to be moved and instructed by them is among our defining gifts. But the narrative habit has a shadow, which lies in its very power to make things intelligible. For the world does not always conform to the shape of a story; much of what happens is the product of impersonal forces, accident and statistical regularity, owing nothing to anyone's purpose and bearing no meaning at all. Yet the narrative mind, hungry for agents and intentions, insists on reading purpose into the purposeless, seeing design where there is only chance, and assigning to events a significance they do not possess. The same faculty that grants us understanding thus tempts us, continually, into a characteristic error: the imposition of story upon a reality that is, in large part, not a story. To use this great gift wisely is to know its limits — to tell our stories while remembering that not everything true can be told as one.

questions.prompt: How do human beings chiefly understand the world, according to the writer?
options: Through stories — narratives of events linked by cause and motive.

questions.prompt: What is the 'largely benign' power of narrative?
options: It renders experience intelligible and transmits wisdom across generations.

questions.prompt: What is the 'shadow' of the narrative habit?
options: That it reads purpose into the purposeless, seeing design where there is only chance.
That it is too weak to matter.

questions.prompt: What does using the gift 'wisely' require?
options: Knowing its limits — remembering that not everything true can be told as a story.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 人主要靠故事理解世界)、细节(B 叙事使经验可解、跨代传递智慧、largely benign)、细节(B 阴影:把目的读入无目的、于纯属偶然处见设计)、主旨(B 智慧使用须知其限——并非一切真相都能讲成故事)。

sets.passage: We are inclined to take the order of our societies for granted, as though the peace and regularity of ordinary life were the natural condition of things, requiring no explanation and in no danger of failing. This assumption is among the most dangerous we make, for the order on which civilised life depends is not natural but artificial — a precarious achievement, maintained by effort and convention, and far more fragile than its apparent solidity suggests. Consider how much of the security we enjoy rests not on force but on habit and mutual expectation: on the countless conventions, observed without thought, by which strangers refrain from preying on one another, honour their agreements, and accept the restraints that make cooperation possible. These conventions have no existence apart from the continued willingness of people to observe them; they are sustained by a kind of collective faith, and they can dissolve with alarming speed when that faith is withdrawn. History offers grim instruction here. The descent of an ordered society into chaos — the unravelling of the restraints that seemed permanent, the swift emergence, beneath the thin crust of civilisation, of cruelties that the settled order had held in check — has occurred too often to be dismissed as an aberration. The veneer of civilisation, it turns out, is thinner than we like to believe, and the forces it contains are never abolished but only suppressed, ready to reassert themselves when the suppressing order weakens. This is not a counsel of despair but of vigilance. The order that protects us is a human creation, not a law of nature; it must be continually maintained, defended and renewed, and it can be lost. To understand this is to value what we have more justly, and to guard more carefully against the complacency that, mistaking a fragile achievement for a permanent possession, is the first step towards its loss. To keep a civilisation in good repair is therefore the unglamorous, unending work of every generation, never finished and easily neglected; and the first symptom of its decline is precisely the complacency that, taking the achievements of the past for a possession that maintains itself, quietly stops doing the work on which their survival depends.

questions.prompt: What 'dangerous assumption' does the writer warn against?
options: That social order is natural, requiring no effort and in no danger of failing.

questions.prompt: On what does much of our security rest, according to the writer?
options: On habit and mutual expectation — conventions sustained by collective faith.
On the abolition of all dangerous forces.

questions.prompt: What 'grim instruction' does history offer?
options: That ordered societies have descended into chaos too often to be dismissed as aberration.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's counsel?
options: Vigilance — the order is a human creation that must be maintained, defended and renewed.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 危险假设:社会秩序是自然的、无需努力且不会失败)、细节(B 安全多靠习惯与相互预期、由集体信念维系)、细节(B 历史警示:有序社会陷入混乱太频繁、非偶然)、主旨/态度(B 劝诫为警醒——秩序是人造之物须不断维护防自满)。

sets.passage: Of all the dispositions that distinguish the human mind, none is more characteristic, or more easily undervalued, than curiosity — the restless desire to know, to understand, to penetrate the why and the how of things, pursued for the sheer satisfaction of understanding and quite apart from any use. We tend to treat curiosity as a charming trait of children, to be tolerated in the young and gently outgrown, or else as a means to practical ends, valued for the discoveries it yields rather than as a good in itself. Both attitudes miss its true significance. For curiosity, rightly understood, is not a childish phase nor a mere instrument but one of the deepest sources of human dignity and joy. It expresses a refusal to be content with the surface of things, an unwillingness to accept the world as a brute given to be used and not understood; and the satisfaction it seeks — the pure pleasure of comprehension, the delight of a mystery resolved or a connection grasped — is among the most distinctively human of pleasures, available to no creature that cannot ask why. A life without curiosity, however comfortable, is diminished in a way its possessor may not even perceive, walled within the familiar, incurious about the vast and astonishing reality that presses on every side. And curiosity has a moral dimension easily overlooked: it is the natural enemy of dogmatism and prejudice, for the genuinely curious mind, hungry to understand, cannot rest in the comfortable certainties that incuriosity protects. To cultivate curiosity, then, is to cultivate not merely a useful trait but a whole orientation towards existence — one of openness, wonder and humility before a world that exceeds our understanding, and that rewards, endlessly, the desire to understand it better. The death of curiosity is a kind of premature death of the mind, and its preservation, through a long life, among the surest marks of one that remains fully alive.

questions.prompt: How do we 'tend to treat' curiosity, according to the writer?
options: As the deepest source of dignity.
As a childish trait to be outgrown, or a mere means to practical ends.
As a moral enemy of prejudice.
As a pure good in itself.

questions.prompt: What does curiosity 'express', in the writer's view?
options: Contentment with the surface of things.
A refusal to accept the world as a brute given to be used and not understood.

questions.prompt: What 'moral dimension' of curiosity does the writer note?
options: It is the natural enemy of dogmatism and prejudice.

questions.prompt: What does the writer say 'the death of curiosity' is?
options: A kind of premature death of the mind.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 我们倾向把好奇当作该长大褪去的童趣、或纯工具)、细节(B 好奇表达拒绝把世界当只可用不可解之物)、细节(B 道德维度:好奇是教条与偏见的天敌)、主旨(B 好奇之死是心智的早夭、其保存是仍鲜活之标志)。
